file_name: ClickstreamCorp_20200330_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_12089935_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Development Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: The Developer agrees to perform the Services hereunder solely as an independent contractor. The Parties agree that nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as creating a joint venture, partnership, franchise, agency, employer/employee, or similar relationship between the Parties, or as authorizing either Party to act as the agent of the other.
perturbation.changed_text: The Developer agrees to perform the Services hereunder as an independent contractor, however, Client retains right of control on Developer's methodology. The Parties agree that nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as creating a joint venture, partnership, franchise, agency, employer/employee, or similar relationship between the Parties, or as authorizing either Party to act as the agent of the other.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the client retains 'right of control on Developer's methodology' contradicts the definition of an independent contractor as they should be free from the client's control over how the service is performed.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) distinguishes between employees and independent contractors. Employees are subject to the employer's control, while independent contractors are not. This modification blurs that line by granting the client control over the developer's methodology, suggesting an employer-employee relationship that contradicts the stated intent of the agreement, therefore violating the FLSA.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 'right of control on Developer's methodology,' which directly contradicts the concept of an independent contractor. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) distinguishes between employees and independent contractors, where employees are subject to the employer's control, but independent contractors are not. The modified text grants the client control over the developer's methodology, suggesting an employer-employee relationship, which goes against the original intent of the agreement to treat the developer as an independent contractor. Although the scraped law snippet is empty, the perturbation explanation and law explanation strongly suggest that the modified contract is not aligned with the definition of an independent contractor.

perturbation.original_text: The Developer hereby warrants and represents that following delivery of the Application System to the Client (which shall be deeded to occur only on the date the Web Application is uploaded to the AWS for distribution) pursuant to Exhibit A (the "Support Period"), the Application will be free from programming errors and defects in workmanship and materials, and will conform to the specifications of Exhibit A. If programming errors or other defects are discovered during the Support Period, the Developer shall promptly remedy those errors or defects at its own expense. The developer will fix any bugs that may come up from the original contract after the 90 days warranty has passed.
perturbation.changed_text: The Developer hereby warrants and represents that following delivery of the Application System to the Client (which shall be deeded to occur only on the date the Web Application is uploaded to the AWS for distribution) pursuant to Exhibit A (the "Support Period"), the Application will be reasonably free from programming errors and defects in workmanship and materials, and will mostly conform to the specifications of Exhibit A. If programming errors or other defects are discovered during the Support Period, the Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to remedy those errors or defects at its own expense. The developer will fix any bugs that may come up from the original contract after the 90 days warranty has passed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be free' to 'will be reasonably free' and 'will conform' to 'will mostly conform' and 'shall promptly remedy' to 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts to remedy' weakens the warranty and introduces ambiguity. 'Commercially reasonable efforts' is subjective and could be interpreted to mean less than a full remedy, and 'reasonably free' is open to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 implies a warranty of merchantability in the sale of goods. Modifying the warranty to 'reasonably free' and 'mostly conform' could be interpreted as a disclaimer of the implied warranty of merchantability without explicitly using the term 'merchantability' and making the disclaimer conspicuous, potentially violating the UCC requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'will be free' to 'reasonably free' and 'will conform' to 'mostly conform' weakens the warranty provided. This directly relates to the UCC § 2-314 which states that goods must 'conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any'. By weakening the warranty, the changed text contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability. The original text provided a stronger guarantee, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with UCC requirements.

perturbation.original_text: All intellectual property developed pursuant to this Agreement before the Termination Date shall be delivered to the Client within one day of the Termination Date.
perturbation.changed_text: All intellectual property developed pursuant to this Agreement before the Termination Date shall be delivered to the Client within a reasonable time of the Termination Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'one day' to 'a reasonable time' introduces ambiguity about the deadline for delivering intellectual property. What constitutes a 'reasonable time' is subjective and could lead to disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.309 - Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.309(1) implies that when a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration, it is valid for a reasonable time. While this section primarily concerns duration, the principle of 'reasonable time' should apply to specific performance obligations within the contract. By replacing the specific deadline of 'one day' with 'a reasonable time,' the modified text introduces uncertainty and potential conflict as to the expected delivery timeframe, potentially contradicting the intent for clear and prompt transfer of intellectual property rights upon termination, potentially violating general contract law principles regarding definiteness of terms.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 309 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 309 Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. F.S. 672.309 672.309 Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. — (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-309, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a definite deadline ('one day') with an ambiguous term ('a reasonable time'). Florida Statutes § 672.309(1) states that 'the time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' While this statute allows for 'reasonable time' when no specific time is agreed upon, the original contract specified 'one day.' By changing this, the modified text introduces ambiguity and potential disputes, contradicting the original intent for a clear deadline and potentially making the contract unenforceable due to vagueness regarding a key performance obligation.
